input.prompt:
We migrated our test project from VSTest to Microsoft.Testing.Platform.
Now our CI pipeline fails with exit code 8 even though no tests actually
failed. Some test assemblies have conditional compilation and produce zero
tests on certain configurations. With VSTest this was fine. How do I fix
this behavioral difference?

data.content:
We migrated our test project from VSTest to Microsoft.Testing.Platform.
Now our CI pipeline fails with exit code 8 even though no tests actually
failed. Some test assemblies have conditional compilation and produce zero
tests on certain configurations. With VSTest this was fine. How do I fix
this behavioral difference?
data.transformedContent:
<current_datetime>2026-04-05T00:49:15.835Z</current_datetime>

tests on certain configurations. With VSTest this was fine. How do I fix
this behavioral difference?

input.initialPrompt:
We migrated our test project from VSTest to Microsoft.Testing.Platform.
Now our CI pipeline fails with exit code 8 even though no tests actually
failed. Some test assemblies have conditional compilation and produce zero
tests on certain configurations. With VSTest this was fine. How do I fix
this behavioral difference?

data.reasoningText:
The user migrated from VSTest to Microsoft.Testing.Platform (MTP) and is getting exit code 8 when test assemblies produce zero tests. This is a known behavioral difference — MTP returns exit code 8 when no tests are discovered, whereas VSTest treated that as success.

Let me look at their project files to understand their setup, and also invoke the relevant skill.

 TestProject.csproj

data.content:
This is a well-known MTP behavioral difference. **Exit code 8** means "zero tests discovered," which MTP treats as an error by default (VSTest did not).

The fix is to tell MTP that zero discovered tests is acceptable by setting the minimum expected tests to `0`.

Add this property to your `.csproj`:

arguments.new_str:
<EnableMSTestRunner>true</EnableMSTestRunner>
    <TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>true</TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>
    <!-- Allow zero tests so conditional-compilation configs don't fail CI -->
    <TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>--minimum-expected-tests 0</TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>

data.reasoningText:
The issue is clear. With MTP, when no tests are discovered (because `INCLUDE_SLOW_TESTS` is not defined), the runner returns exit code 8, which is the "no tests found" exit code. VSTest used to return 0 in this case.

The fix is to set the MSBuild property `TestingPlatformShowTestsFailure` to `false` — wait, that's not right. Let me think about this.

Actually, the correct property for MTP to not fail on zero tests is to configure the "no tests found" behavior. In MTP, there's a specific option for this.

For Microsoft.Testing.Platform, the relevant setting is:


The `--minimum-expected-tests 0` command line option, which I can pass through `dotnet test -- --minimum-expected-tests 0`, or I can configure it in the project file or runsettings. The exit code 8 specifically means no tests were found, so I need to either suppress that failure or adjust the minimum expected test count to zero.

arguments.new_str:
<EnableMSTestRunner>true</EnableMSTestRunner>
    <TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>true</TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>
    <!-- Allow zero tests so conditional-compilation configs don't fail CI -->
    <TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>--minimum-expected-tests 0</TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>

    <TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>true</TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>", "new_str": "    <EnableMSTestRunner>true</EnableMSTestRunner>
    <TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>true</TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>
    <!-- Allow zero tests so conditional-compilation configs don't fail CI -->
    <TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>--minimum-expected-tests 0</TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>"}

data.reasoningText: It seems like the `--minimum-expected-tests 0` argument isn't being picked up via `TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments`, or there's a different issue. Let me check the log file for more details.

arguments.description: Check test log for error details

toolArgs.description: Check test log for error details

data.content: The `--minimum-expected-tests` option rejects `0`. But MTP provides `--ignore-exit-code` specifically for this. Exit code 8 = "no tests found". Let me use that instead:

arguments.old_str:
<!-- Allow zero tests so conditional-compilation configs don't fail CI -->
    <TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>--minimum-expected-tests 0</TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>
arguments.new_str:
<!-- Exit code 8 = zero tests discovered; safe to ignore for conditional-compilation configs -->
    <TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>--ignore-exit-code 8</TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>

data.reasoningText:
The error says `--minimum-expected-tests` expects a "single non-zero positive integer value". So `0` is not accepted. But looking at the help output, there's another option: `--ignore-exit-code` which can ignore specific exit codes like 8.

Let me use `--ignore-exit-code 8` instead.

arguments.old_str:
<!-- Allow zero tests so conditional-compilation configs don't fail CI -->
    <TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>--minimum-expected-tests 0</TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>
arguments.new_str:
<!-- Exit code 8 = zero tests discovered; safe to ignore for conditional-compilation configs -->
    <TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>--ignore-exit-code 8</TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>

data.content:
**The fix:** Add `--ignore-exit-code 8` via the `<TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>` MSBuild property.

**What happened:** MTP returns exit code **8** when zero tests are discovered — unlike VSTest which returned 0. Since your `#if INCLUDE_SLOW_TESTS` conditionally compiles away all tests in some configurations, MTP flags this as a failure.

The `--ignore-exit-code 8` option tells MTP to return 0 instead of 8, restoring the VSTest behavior. Actual test failures (exit code 2) are still reported normally.

If you have multiple test projects, you can put this in a shared `Directory.Build.props` instead.
